perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees that for a period of twenty (20) days from the date of the Refinancing Meeting, unless specifically consented to in writing by Blueknight, Receiving Party and its controlled Affiliates will not, directly or indirectly, effect any sale (including any short-sale) or acquisition of any equity securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or acquisition of assets of Blueknight or any of its Affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees that for a period of five (5) days from the date of the Refinancing Meeting, unless specifically consented to in writing by Blueknight, Receiving Party and its controlled Affiliates will not, directly or indirectly, effect any sale (including any short-sale) or acquisition of any equity securities (or beneficial ownership thereof) or acquisition of assets of Blueknight or any of its Affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the restricted trading period to 5 days could potentially violate insider trading regulations, as it may not provide sufficient time for material non-public information to become public knowledge and be properly disseminated.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits trading on material non-public information. While the rule doesn't explicitly define a blackout period, a 5-day restriction may be insufficient to ensure that information disclosed at the Refinancing Meeting becomes public and allows for informed trading decisions, especially if the information is complex or requires significant analysis. This heightened risk increases chances of illegal insider trading.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the restricted trading period from 20 to 5 days, potentially violating Rule 10b-5, which prohibits trading on material non-public information. The original text provided a longer period which is more safe from insider trading accusations. Even though Rule 10b-5 doesn't explicitly define a blackout period, shortening it to 5 days increases the risk that information from the Refinancing Meeting won't become public knowledge in time for informed trading decisions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, states it is unlawful to employ any device to defraud in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation make it clear that shortening the blackout period heightens the risk of illegal insider trading.

perturbation.original_text: Blueknight and the General Partner agree that for a period of twenty (20) days from the date of the Refinancing Meeting, unless specifically consented to in writing by Receiving Party, Blueknight will not file a proxy statement with the Securities and Exchange Commission relating to the Unitholder Meeting (as defined in the Global Transaction Agreement, dated as of October 25, 2010, by and among the General Partner, Blueknight and the purchasers set forth in Schedule I thereto).
perturbation.changed_text: Blueknight and the General Partner agree that for a period of five (5) days from the date of the Refinancing Meeting, unless specifically consented to in writing by Receiving Party, Blueknight will not file a proxy statement with the Securities and Exchange Commission relating to the Unitholder Meeting (as defined in the Global Transaction Agreement, dated as of October 25, 2010, by and among the General Partner, Blueknight and the purchasers set forth in Schedule I thereto).
perturbation.explanation: A 5-day restriction on filing a proxy statement after a meeting where material information is discussed could violate SEC regulations regarding timely and accurate disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 14a-3 requires that shareholders receive a proxy statement before a solicitation. Reducing the time Blueknight waits to file a proxy statement to 5 days after a refinancing meeting may prevent adequate reflection of refinancing changes in the proxy and insufficient time for shareholders to receive, review, and act on material information before the unitholder meeting, thus violating the intent and purpose of the Securities Exchange Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.14a-3 - Information to be furnished to security holders. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.14a-3 - Information to be furnished to security holders. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.14a-3 Information to be furnished to security holders. (a) No solicitation subject to this regulation shall be made unless each person solicited is concurrently furnished or has previously been furnished with: (1) A publicly-filed preliminary or definitive proxy statement , in the form and manner described in § 240.14a-16 , containing the information specified in Schedule 14A ( § 240.14a-101 ); (2) A preliminary or definitive written proxy statement included in a registration statement filed under the Securities Act of 1933 on Form S-4 or F-4 ( § 239.25 or § 239.34 of this chapter) or Form N-14 ( § 239.23 of this chapter) and containing the information specified in such Form; or (3) A publicly-filed preliminary or definitive proxy statement , not in the form and manner described in § 240.14a-16 , containing the information specified in Schedule 14A ( § 240.14a-101 ), if: (i) The solicitation relates to a business combination transaction as defined in § 230.165 of this chapter, as well as transactions for cash consideration requiring disclosure under Item 14 of § 240.14a-101; or (ii) The solicitation may not follow the form and manner described in § 240.14a-16 pursuant to the laws of the state of incorporation of the registrant . (b) If the solicitation is made on behalf of the registrant , other than an investment company registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 , and relates to an annual (or special meeting in lieu of the annual) meeting of security holders, or written consent in lieu of such meeting, at which directors are to be elected, each proxy statement furnished pursuant to paragraph (a) of this section shall be accompanied or p
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time Blueknight has to file a proxy statement from 20 days to 5 days, which could violate SEC regulations regarding timely and accurate disclosure. The Law Explanation states that this reduction may prevent adequate reflection of refinancing changes in the proxy, insufficient time for shareholders to receive, review, and act on material information, thus violating the intent and purpose of the Securities Exchange Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (17 CFR § 240.14a-3) states that 'No solicitation subject to this regulation shall be made unless each person solicited is concurrently furnished or has previously been furnished with... A publicly-filed preliminary or definitive proxy statement'. By shortening the time to 5 days, the changed text risks not furnishing the proxy statement in a timely manner, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and the obligations of the parties hereunder (other than Section 4(b)) shall terminate upon the earlier to occur of (i) twenty (20) days from the date of the Refinancing Meeting, (ii) such time as Receiving Party is no longer in possession of Blueknight Non-public Information and (iii) with respect to any Section of this Agreement (other than Section 4(b)), such earlier time as set forth in such Section.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and the obligations of the parties hereunder (other than Section 4(b)) shall terminate upon the earlier to occur of (i) five (5) days from the date of the Refinancing Meeting, (ii) such time as Receiving Party is no longer in possession of Blueknight Non-public Information and (iii) with respect to any Section of this Agreement (other than Section 4(b)), such earlier time as set forth in such Section.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 5 days from the Refinancing Meeting might not be sufficient to protect Blueknight's non-public information, potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets. If the 'Blueknight Non-public Information' includes trade secrets, a 5-day confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to prevent misappropriation, especially if the information requires time to analyze, implement, or exploit. This increases chances of trade secret misappropriation which is what DTSA is designed to protect against.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from 20 days to 5 days. The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation, aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. The Perturbation Explanation argues that a 5-day confidentiality period may be insufficient to protect Blueknight's non-public information, especially if it includes trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings for violations related to the protection of trade secrets. Because the reduced period increases the risk of misappropriation, and thus a violation of trade secret protections, a contradiction exists as it undermines the purpose of the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Section 4(b) of this Agreement and the obligations of the parties under Section 4(b) shall terminate upon the date that is ten (10) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 4(b) of this Agreement and the obligations of the parties under Section 4(b) shall terminate upon the date that is one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the duration of the agreement concerning the inadmissibility of settlement discussions to one year could be insufficient to protect Blueknight's legal position and may contradict legal principles regarding the admissibility of settlement negotiations in future proceedings, potentially violating legal privilege.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Evidence 408 protects settlement negotiations from being used as evidence of liability. While the rule doesn't mandate a specific duration for confidentiality, limiting the agreement to only one year may not adequately safeguard Blueknight's interests in potential future litigation, especially if legal proceedings extend beyond that timeframe. Disclosing details of settlement discussions just after a year reduces the protections that Rule 408 should provide. Note that NY state law follows the same principle as outlined in FRE 408.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 408. Compromise Offers and Negotiations | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 408. Compromise Offers and Negotiations (a) Prohibited Uses. Evidence of the following is not admissible — on behalf of any party — either to prove or disprove the validity or amount of a disputed claim or to impeach by a prior inconsistent statement or a contradiction: (1) furnishing, promising, or offering — or accepting, promising to accept, or offering to accept — a valuable consideration in compromising or attempting to compromise the claim; and (2) conduct or a statement made during compromise negotiations about the claim — except when offered in a criminal case and when the negotiations related to a claim by a public office in the exercise of its regulatory, investigative, or enforcement authority. (b) Exceptions. The court may admit this evidence for another purpose, such as proving a witness’s bias or prejudice, negating a contention of undue delay, or proving an effort to obstruct a criminal investigation or prosecution. Notes (Pub. L. 93–595, §1, Jan. 2, 1975, 88 Stat. 1933; Apr. 12, 2006, eff. Dec. 1, 2006; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Proposed Rules As a matter of general agreement, evidence of an offer-to compromise a claim is not receivable in evidence as an admission of, as the case may be, the validity or invalidity of the claim. As with evidence of subsequent remedial measures, dealt with in Rule 407, exclusion may be based on two grounds. (1) The evidence is irrelevant, since the offer may be motivated by a desire for peace rather than from any concession of weakness of position. The validity of this position will vary as the amount of the offer varies in relation to the size of the claim and may also be influenced by other circumstances. (2) a more consistently impressive ground is promotion of the public policy favoring the compromise and settlement
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the duration of protection for settlement discussions from ten years to one year. The LLM explanation and Perturbation explanation outline that this shorter duration may not adequately protect Blueknight's interests, potentially violating legal privilege. Federal Rule of Evidence 408, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, protects settlement negotiations from being used as evidence of liability. While Rule 408 doesn't specify a minimum duration, the significant reduction in the protection period undermines the intent of the rule to safeguard settlement discussions, particularly if litigation extends beyond one year. This inconsistency between the reduced protection period and the purpose of Rule 408 creates a legal contradiction. The change introduces a significant weakening of the protection that Rule 408 aims to provide, creating a direct and serious contradiction.
